does: This paper introduces Multilingual Self-Distillation (MSD), a framework that transfers safety capabilities from high-resource languages like English to low-resource languages in large language models without requiring target-language response data. The authors implement two concrete variants—on-policy MSD and off-policy MSD—that leverage only multilingual queries to achieve cross-lingual safety transfer. They additionally propose Dual-Perspective Safety Weighting (DPSW), a divergence measure designed to optimize the distillation objective by jointly considering multiple safety perspectives. The framework demonstrates that LLMs' existing safety mechanisms can be leveraged across languages without expensive translation or annotation of safety-critical responses in each target language.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap exposed by recent research on multilingual vulnerabilities in LLMs, building on prior findings that safety alignment is language-dependent and that low-resource languages suffer disproportionately from jailbreak susceptibility. It extends the self-distillation paradigm—where models learn from their own outputs—to the cross-lingual setting, adapting techniques from knowledge distillation and transfer learning literature. The research responds to the practical limitation of existing safety alignment methods that require high-quality curated data in each target language, building on RLHF and related supervision-based approaches while offering a more scalable alternative grounded in the model's inherent multilingual capabilities.
enables: This framework enables practical safety alignment for hundreds of low-resource languages without the expensive requirement of generating safety training data in each language. It opens research directions into understanding and exploiting cross-lingual safety transfer mechanisms—why safety properties transfer between languages and under what conditions they degrade. The work could facilitate development of universally safer multilingual models and points toward more general approaches to distributing alignment properties across model capabilities. Future work might extend these techniques to other safety-critical properties, combine distillation with other alignment methods, or investigate theoretical foundations of cross-lingual safety transfer to further improve coverage and robustness across linguistic diversity.
